speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four. Today is January 2, 1997. I'm interviewing Shirley Warman. I'd like to talk more about the climate in Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said in the summer it was very, very hot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea what the temperatures were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- but it's very, very hot. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- you said also about ants and other insects.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the very hot climate, and it's-- it was filthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many ants? I mean, how did they attack people--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or how do they--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they came to you. They're all on your face, and your body, you know, it's-- but in the evening, it got cooler. It just the middle of the day got very hot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- are the-- any other bugs that used to come out?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- listen. You-- when you-- when you're near a forest, they all kind of, you know, cames out-- bugs.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were also near water, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The water was far from us. It was far. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they swamps, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We never go-- went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And during the winter, can you really tell us what it was like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the winter it was very, very cold. You couldn't go out, but you have to. You went to work. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You mentioned before that there were long days in the-- in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the summertime, there was very long days. And the-- and the night was short. In the wintertime, was the nights longer and the days were shorter. But it was dark. It's not it was shorter day. Start-- you know, we went to work when it was dark, and we came home it was dark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any elderly people in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What arrangements were made for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They were-- they had families-- wives and children-- not many, maybe about 10 couples. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were there any children in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they treated by--

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing. There was no difference. They didn't work. The children didn't work, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any episodes that happened to children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where any babies born in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that the people that you were with in the camp were Russians from Russia--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who were wealthy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Can you tell us a little bit about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing to tell. You know, they was nice to us. Not many, but they didn't live-- many go near us. But we were talking. First of all, I didn't understand their language. So it was no use to say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Polish people, you know, Jews? Are there--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we don't-- I don't know if there-- I think they was Russian. Maybe they were Jew Russian. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Polish people that came with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but not many-- not many. Yes. They was captured the same way like we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were those the most people that you communicated with-- the Polish people that were there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were together. When they went out, we saw each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge in the-- in the-- from the Russians? Where were their headquarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same place. It's-- yeah. It was a big building-- not too tall. But that's a nice building. It was warm there. You know, and it was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the building made of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not from brick-- stucco-- not from brick, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the guards there? They're from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have any special uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were-- their uniforms what the soldiers wearing, the Russian soldiers. Now, a lot of them was already from us, from the Jewish people. They're picked. They should-- yeah, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're saying what? That the Russians picked some Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They should help supervise. Yes, they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and who did they pick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was their role in-- in-- as supervisor?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're taught to talk to us. We should work. We should survive. And they showed us how. And they watched us-- you know, certain things. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they nice to the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were OK. They were all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. On the part of the Russian guards, did they show any anti-Semitism--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point in time, when you were in this first camp in Siberia, were you aware of what was going on in Europe and other places?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Yes, we knew. One friend of mine became a letter from a Polish-- his parents ask a Polish guy, he should send a letter. The letter went maybe six months. And there was nothing there. It just said-- it was-- everything was lie. The parents, they were OK. They have food. But this wasn't true.

speaker: interviewer
text: For you couldn't go through the-- they looked over, you know, how do you call? The sensors. No, they--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know. Nothing else. We didn't know anything what's going on. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the effects of life in Siberia on you at that time? What was the worst thing that was happening to you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The worst thing, I was all alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very bad. I didn't have what to eat. I didn't have what to wear. I didn't know if I'm human. That-- it just-- the lice ate me up. And I had a habit. I had my piece of bread. When they give you, let's assume at noon, I saved a little piece. I couldn't go to sleep. When I was laying already on this-- on the barracks-- as I put the little piece of bread into my-- into the straw, when I go to sleep, I should take this in my mouth. And I should chew a lot. Then I fall asleep. And then one day I did it. But somebody else, he ate it up. A mouse, he ate up the piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I didn't have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: I slept. I was tired. I slept, but I was hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other things happened to your physical-- your body?

speaker: interviewee
text: My body-- what happened? I didn't have my period. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your eyesight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, where-- I-- I was-- you know, about-- about 200 hundred people was walking. And we couldn't see-- comes in the evening. One person took like a rope and they tied us up, and we're walking from one place to the other. It came in the morning, my eyesight came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. People said the malnutrition or something. I don't know. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people get any other illnesses in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody pays attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said earlier that you thought more about your parents than your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you think when you thought of your parents? What were your reactions to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I left them-- I shouldn't go. I should be with them. What have happened to them? What happened to me? I feel very-- I felt sorry. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you dream about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I dreamt just when I supposed to have my baby. I dreamt of my father. I didn't dream of anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The guards in the camp, when you left, what did they say-- where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were-- you were in the first camp in Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I guess I should say, how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stay, I don't know. We stay a year or more. I don't really know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was warm already. I did-- we didn't really-- they took us out!

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a pact, the Russian with the Polish, that they let us out from there to Siberia-- Tomsk-- to Tomsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was another place in Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, but over there it was already like warm and cold. It was not so hot. It was very nice. We came there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, well, let me just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This is-- you know, then-- then I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't know where we you going. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We take-- we take us-- we take-- to us out to this place. We're gonna-- we-- you're gonna go in a better place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were all the Jews taken out, or was it other people too?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole people-- not the Russian what there was there before. No, not them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many people went?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people, a lot. It was a big train, a big train. It was a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a train or a cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cattle cars-- the cattle cars. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I was wearing! Nothing else! I have nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Food? This was a luxury. Whatever they give you, you ate right away up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions on-- on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was no conditions-- nothing there. In middle of the night, they open the doors. They brought you bread and water. And they let you go into the trays what somebody has to make. A lot of people got cut off their arms. The train start to go, we-- and they didn't-- they couldn't go up.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had gotten off the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got-- we went to the train to help ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not everybody could make it in the-- in the-- in the-- in the-- the train. There was a hole. Everybody was looking! A lot of people we went-- we went into the train on the rails. And we didn't know the train start to move. A lot of people cut off their arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were they treated? Were they given medical--

speaker: interviewee
text: They took them away. I don't know. Everybody was selfish. Everybody was for themselves. I said, thanks God that's not me. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what they were saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said the same thing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything unusual happen on the way to Tomsk--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- yes. Where the train stops-- you know, in every village, the train stopped. And trains came from other places. You know, there was the coal mines whenever there. And the train stops. And everybody was anxious, you know, to look up, maybe to find somebody. I always was thinking, maybe I'm gonna find a brother or somebody. And I looked out, and I saw a guy was standing across this train. And he recognized me. I didn't recognize him.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, [NON-ENGLISH], are-- is this you? I said, who are you? I said, I'm Shmulek. I lived in, you know, in-- in-- not far from your-- from Benny's-- even-- from Benny's parents. And Benny was--

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, Benjamin-- Ben-- on [NON-ENGLISH] street! I said, oh, my God. I start to scream. I start to cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other people start to push me away. They wanna look, maybe they'll-- he's telling me, you know who's with me? I said, who is with me? My brother? He said, no. It's Benjamin. It's your husband, Benny. And-- and they were where? In another train?

speaker: interviewee
text: In another train, like the opposite we're sitting. I said, it's cannot be. It's not true. He pushed him away. He looked. I didn't recognize-- you saw the little picture. I didn't recognize him. But I couldn't go to him. He couldn't go to me. The guard didn't let nobody out. But I told him, look. Middle of the night when they bring the-- the bread, with, you know, the food, you try to sneak in here. You just say you went into the train. And like this he came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the way to Tomsk--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Tomsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your husband snuck from his train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Here, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reunion like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing-- no emotional, no feelings, nothing-- like two strange people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had he been, your husband? Where was he coming from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came from-- from the coal mines. I don't know how to call it, you know. It's a better place, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had he been? Was he in Siberia too?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, but it's a different part, you know. Over there, they were-- they were working coal mines-- Kazakhstan or something. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he look when you saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- not human. He was in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was he in jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: He start talk to people. Maybe we should run away. Maybe we should do something. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you saw him on the train, did you talk at all when you met him finally, when he came to your train?

speaker: interviewee
text: We talk. Most of all, we talk about our families, not about us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: About his family, and about my family. And I said, if they survive, if some day we're gonna see them. That's it. That's was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get to Tomsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think 1942. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you had met this brief time, he went back to his train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was with me all the time already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. So you were reunited on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. When you got there, what did you first see when the doors were opened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Tomsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: They let us go! You're free. You could go wherever you wanted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who said that to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guard said you are free. You in Siberia, in Tomsk. And everybody, you wanna go, you have to go look for work. You have to go look where to sleep. And so you've got to survive. A lot of people went to Tashkent in the warm climate. And they all got typhus. And they all died from the cold weather to the hot. And they switch. Maybe it was our luck we went to Tomsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Tomsk in Siberia? What part of Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the weather like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nice. It was cold. In the summertime it was very nicely. In the winter, it's cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the same climate as the first camp that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It wasn't below zero, no, no, no. It was cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you from the first camp to get to Tomsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weeks-- weeks, yes. We stepped up. We stopped off, you know, at a lot of villages. And a lot of Russian people came to see us. And they was thinking we have something for them to give. We were-- we will ask them. They should give us. They were friendly, who could speak with them. Not everybody could. I couldn't speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened in Tomsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomsk, they put us-- we went down from the train. And we didn't know where to go. There was no place where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was walking with him. And I sat on a-- on a-- on the curb in the street. And I was crying. I said, what we gonna do? Where are we gonna go?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: One Russian passed by. He was a lieutenant or a major-- I don't know-- from Moscow. He came over. He said, why are you crying?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I have nowhere to go. And I have nowhere to sleep. And I'm hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said to me like this, if-- I have a brother here. He has a big house, but you got to work there. You got to work in the garden. You clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said-- I said-- I said to him, can he come too? This I said. This is my husband. He said, yes. He came there and took us in. First of all, they give us to get cleaned up. Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian-- Russian family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was-- there was-- there was, you know, very big people for in Moscow. And where the-- the war, you know, the occupied-- the German occupied. Most were the-- this, and they came here. And they give us clothing. And they give us food. And they told us, you have to-- you're gonna go in in the stable to sleep there. It's nice and warm. And they showed us what to work. We worked there on the garden. We cleaned the house. We washed the clothes. But over there we couldn't stay too long out there. There were military people, and they take your-- they send them away someplace else. So we went out. And then, you know, you start to talk. You find people. So we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was like a house. It was-- everybody was sleeping there. Like over here, the-- the people what they have no place to go-- how you call those? The homeless.

speaker: interviewee
text: The homeless. Over there was Russians too. And we were-- we came there. It was warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what month it was and what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1942-- '42. It was in the summertime. It was not too bad. And we start to talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one guy came. He said, who wants to go to work on construction work? I said, I will.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to my husband he said-- he was so skinny. So he said, you gonna, you know, we have a place where to cook for the workers, for the school-- a kitchen. He tell them over there to work. And with me, I was working in construction work. I mixed cement with my hands, you know. And I-- and I took the brick on my shoulders to the 5th floor. They was building a house, on the-- on a-- in a step-- in a ladder it took. And I was working there. It was OK. And I had food. Then my husband brought home more food. But we share over there. And you can't just sit down and eat. The other people didn't have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you working? For whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a special company?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Over there, everybody is from the-- from the-- from the-- from the government. There's no private enterprise. And I worked. And then we were digging holes to make to put the pipes in. And over there, there were no men. The men was on the-- in the army. And a lot of women would worked. Then one woman said, if you wanna have something better, I have a very nice apartment, and you help me out. And I went. It was beautiful-- a big room. We all four slept in one room. I slept on the floor. It was warm. It was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- in-- in Tomsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. In a special part of Tomsk, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle of the-- the-- in the city. I don't know. And like this it went on. That's it. And I always worked then. Then it start to get very bad. The guys what they teach us how to work, and-- and they said, you know, how to-- how to do it. And there was like the supervisors, they to start to get very fresh. If you're not gonna give your body, you can't work here. I left them. I didn't have no work. And they give us, you know, little books for a month. You have to go and get bread-- food-- for bread. My luck was, the minute I got it, the minute they stole it from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: They stole your bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: They stole my whole book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your ration.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ration. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I don't remember. Then I went someplace else to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you get food at that time-- after they stole?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give us rationing. Yeah, they give us food.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. After they stole your ration book.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing! If you have money, you go and buy, you know, food, whatever somebody sells.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get food after they stole that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my husband just worked. I went to work for somebody. I wash clothes. And then, you know, I was working someplace else, in-- in another place. I was the-- in the, the thing-- the-- to put some very heavy pipes. And the pipes was heavy. And then I said, I can't work here. Now I have to go home. And they let me. They didn't bother much. And I said it's cold. And I told him it's very cold in the house. They gave me some wood. And I put the wood on my shoulders, and I went home. And I fell down. And then I got sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pieces of blood came out from me. My stomach was very big. And then I was very big.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your stomach was big from what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, from this thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given medical treatment there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. It came out, and I came home. And I start to wash myself up. I told-- I told the Russian woman what happened. And he said it must be your period, you know, this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the first time till 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever a time that you were lacking in food, that you needed to eat food from like potato peels or-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was-- but this was in-- this was in Siberia. I forgot. You know, we went to the garbage to look when they peeled potatoes over there for the kitchen to cook. So I took out, you know, I went to the garbage, and I picked something frozen to peel from the potatoes. I took it in. And I boiled them. And there was smell. It was stinking. And I drink the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was in the first place in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your reactions to that when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no reaction-- nothing. You felt like you're-- that you're not human anymore. Whatever comes, comes. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Getting back to Tomsk, the-- the situation with food was somewhat better?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your husband had been separated for about a year when the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's more than-- it was more than a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: More than a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came together again, what was your relationship like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No relationship. We didn't have no desire for anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you talking about sexual relations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No desire. No, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till we went to [NON-ENGLISH]. Over there it was already better.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other things happened in Tomsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to get any information about your family or what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. We knew already the war was finished. I mean-- I mean, you know, they said people find out already. 1942, they took all the Jewish people from the ghetto. And they took them to Biysk, and over there they got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what they did with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everybody, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that? How did you find that out exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: People talking, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Tomsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomsk, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did any Jewish life exist in Tomsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't look. We didn't look for it. I don't know. Maybe it was. There was a lot of Jewish people. There was, but where did you find them?

speaker: interviewer
text: What else happened in Tomsk that you want to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband get sick there in Tomsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about you, other than having the blood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had malaria again, you know, I was-- that's it-- nothing else, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated? Medical treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. They give me, you know-- yeah, they treated me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have any hospitals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was hospitals, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Tomsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I left was it-- it was nice-- warm till-- 19-- I think 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or 1944, I think so-- '44. I was there till the war finished. We were over there till 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: But from Tomsk--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Tomsk to-- to Katalovka.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the next tape.
